LOUTH.

There were between 4,000 and s,000.
persons present on Sunday at the Athletic
Grounds, Dundalk, when the semi-final
‘of the Louth Senior-Championship was
decided. The contending teams. were
the Drogheda Tredaghs and Ardee St.
Mochtas, In the opening stages the
Tredaghs attacked, but were’ unsuccess-
ful, and. stiff _mid-feld play followed.
Ardee forced matters, but were repulsed.
After fifteen minutes of a very hard game
the Ardee team opened the scoring’ with
& point, The Tredaghs endeavoured to
equalise, but could not get through the
defence, and Arde had most of the play
henceforth, adding a further point to
their score. Half-time—Ardee, 2 points;
Drogheda, nil,

On turning over, Tredaghs made a hot
attack 6a thelr opponents) defence, and
after a feal of bustling play got
through @ point.” The game way nove a

7 nd the football was
f . The ball travelled
with amazing rapidity from one end to
the-other, and eventually the Tredaghs
equalised. The Ardee men now swept
along: determinedly, and raised the white
flag, amidst great excitement, Tredaghs
replied in no less earnest form, and alter
some unsuccessful shots they again equal-
ised. Both teams were now playing. all
they Knew, but neither of them could
obtain an ‘advantage. A few minutes
from fulltime, during a slight lull due to
fatigue, the Tredaghs, from. a scramble
some distance from goal, succeeded in
getting the ball through the posts, and
the green‘ lag was raised for the frst
time. The Ardee men-forced their way
Through and into the goal-mouth of their
opponents, but the Tredagh custodian
saved narrowly, and his forwards getting
possession, sent clear to the other end—
‘where, amidst great cheering, a further
goal Was shot. ‘The whistle went shortly
Afterwards, leaving the score, in a very
excited game:
~ Tredaghs = 2 goals 3/points.

British supporters were working with all

count for much, and, eventually, they threw
“squashing the G.A.A.” These were,
indeed, the “fighting days” in which i¢
wasa distinction to be in the fray. Excite-
ment and enthusiasm in Irish athletic circles
enlivened the fight for victory. I'he rival
centres were kept well informed by volun.
tary intelligence departments which were
watching events throughout the country,
and every arranged athletic fixture was
awaited with breathless interest until it be-
came known by which side it was to be
controlled. Many hurling and football
matches and some handball contests and
athletic meetings had been already held
under the auspices of the Gaelic Athletic
Association. Toames, a little village near
Macroom, in County Cork, held the
athletic meeting ten days after the founding
of the G,A.A.at Thurles. Great concern
was centred in the Cork Queen’s College
Sports fixture, bat the ‘garrison ” side
succeeded in capturing ¢

was held on March 16th, the eve of the
‘National festival. Popul

the Lee and throughout the county was
very embittered against the action of the
College authorities, and by way of appeasing
the outburst of popular indignation, those

DUBLIN FINALS. |

#
INTERESTING GAME AT CROKE PARK.

On Sunday, before a fine attendance,
the Senior Football and Junior Hurling
Finals.of the Dublin Championship were
played at Croke Park. In football, the
contestants were Parnells and Geraldines,
while two North County teams figured in
the hurling final. Both games were
interesting, but particularly in the open-
ing moieties, when the competition was
.pretty even. :

PARNELLS y. GERALDINES.

- Geraldines assumed the offensive with
‘a-strong wind against them, but could
only send wide. Aided by frees, Parnells
were aggressive, and some exciting play
took place under the Geraldine posts,
Fallon saving a dangerous situation.
Maurice Collins sent Gers. attacking, and
Parker put his side one point ahead with
a long kick.
The Parnell goalkeeper: distinguished
himself in the succeeding play, keeping
his goal intact against some strong
charges by Lawlor and Keane. Crystal
raised the siege, and a fine effort. by
Letmon at the other end went wide.
The play livened up in interest, and
fast end to end play was the order. The
Gers., however, had the better of the
argument, but the splendid defence of
Crystal and M. Manifold kept them from
scoring. Collins, Doyle and Sole led
repeated charges upon the Parnell posts,
but the defence held out. -
A free to Parnells close to the Gers.’
posts was badly handled, but the Sea-
siders returned with a fine run by Letmon,
who was playing a grand game. Kelly
_ saved when a score seemed certain; but
Parnells were not to be denied, Dowdall
sending over the equaliser from a free.

Gers. attacked in force after this, and

Stacey sent to Lawlor—who, on the
second attempt, gave Gers. the lead with
a point—supplemented by a similar score
from a free immediately afterwards. Tfin-
nerty failed at the other end when well
placed, and half-time found the situation :

Geraldines - - 3 points.

Parnells - - _1 point.

_ Geraldines had indisputably the best of

the play in the first half, and looked like

winning. The defence of Parnells was

the best single feature of the game so far,

and the Geraldine centre-field was also
~ conspicuous. <

The resumption found Parnells attack-
ing very strongly, and an assault on the

Farrelly stopped well.

Parnells rallied again, but Kelly re-+

lieved, and from a free on the fourteen
yards’ mark, Lawlor put Geraldines six
points up. Following another strong but

fruitless attack by Parnells, Geraldines

resumed the offensive, and Lawlor scored

the last point of the match. Final score:
Geraldines  - - 7 points.
Parnells - - — I point.

For the winners, Fallon (goal) was
very safe, and in the back line useful help
was afforded by Flood, Kelly and Far-
relly. Stacey, Sole, Lawlor, Parker,
Keane and Doyle were the pick of a good
set of forwards. In the losing ranks,
the full-back line was exccllent—consist-
‘The brothers Manifold, Finnerty and
McDermott were also good. Letmon
was the best forward on the field, with
Lawlor (Gers.) a good second.

The better team won. Mr.
Boland proved an efficient referee.

Harry

LUSK vy. NAUL,

The Junior Hurling Final—Lusk v.
Naul—opened with a strong Lusk attack,
which was without result. The Lusk
goal had a narrow escape, the ball just
glancing off the upright when the goal-
keeper was well beaten. Nice hurling
followed, in Which Lusk had the advan-
tage, and culminating in a point for that
side.

Naul, attacking in turn, were fortunate
in securing a fluky goal. Lusk, playing
neater hurling, were aggressive once
more, but were driven off. Naul scored
an additional point at the other end.
Lusk resumed the offensive, and the left-
winger evened the scoring with a goal
from thirty yards-out. After beating off
further Lusk attacks, Naul broke away
and narrowly missed a goal, but were
compensated a minute afterwards by a
second fluky major, registered by White.

Fast hurling followed, in which the
Lusk centre-field showed up to advan-
tage, but the forwards lacked finish.
They were successful in obtaining a
minor, to which, after repulsing a further
offensive by Naul, a meritorious goal was

added. Half-time score:
Lusk - - 2 goals 2 points.
Naul - - 2 goals 1 point.

Lusk were slightly the superior team
in the opening half, being “ handier ”’
than their opponents, and deserved their
lead. Naul were weightier, but not so
fast.

On resuming, Lusk were quickly on
the aggressive, and ran up a score of
two goals in sharp time. After the Naul
goalkeeper had saved well, the forwards
got moving, and a fluky goal again fell
to their lot. From a melee the leather
was again sent into the Lusk net, but the
score was disallowed.

Lusk again assumed the aggressive,
and, playing a very good game, a fine
goal was brought off by O’Shea. They
continued a strong aggressive, but. the

defence of McDermott was useful in
keeping down the score.
Naul attacked intermittently towards

the finish, but they were outclassed and
failed to increase their total. The better
team won on the final score:
Lusk - - § goals 3 points.
Naul - - 3 goals 1 point.
Mr. John Kirwan was an impartial

referee. -THe Carr.

UGHS v. COLLEGIANS.

The Senior League
Banehs a: tal osrtibsante—r
a moderate attendance.

Faughs were very aggressive in the
early exchanges, and were rewarded with
a rather easy goal. Collegians played
up determinedly, and after some neat
hurling they equalised the scoring. The
Collegians were conducting a strong
aggressive for a period, but they sent
wide frequently. Daly, the Collegian
goalkeeper, distinguished himself at the
other end, where Hogan’s centres were
a source of frequent danger.

Bulfin, Hickey and Lalor put Collegians
in a good position, but Rody and Boland
stopped. ‘The defence was sound on both
sides and the hurling good. The Col-
legian goalkeeper at this period was play-
ing a splendid game.

Faughs were first to assume the Jead,
when Delaney scored a point. After
some keen hurling exchanges they sup-
plemented this by a further goal and a
point.

Collegians rallied in fine style, and tor
some minutes they conducted a strong
attack. Their efforts were eventually
rewarded with a goal, which left them
two points in arrears. Half-time score:

E
Hurling Ties—
ee

Faughs - 2 goals 2 points.
Collegians - 2 goals o points.

The Collegian forwards resumed the
attack on the re-start, and Stewart and
Bourke made good attempts to_ score.
Fine hurling cnsued, but the Faugh’s
defence was supreme, and Boland and
Rody cleared successively. At the other
end, Faughs rushed the leather into the
net, thereby increasing their lead. The
Faughs were now persistent in their
attacks, the good defence of Ryan and
O’Brien being penetrated by Burke for
another goal.

A quick succession of scores followed.
Collegians got moving and sent in a nice
point. Faughs rushed a goal at the
other end, but Collegians returned and
got a further major.

Grand hurling was now the order, the
exchanges being even, and Collegians
livened up considerably. Mockler’s
(Faughs) long drives were of great
advantage to his side.

From a strong Collegian attack, led
by Lawlor, Hic! raised the white flag
for Collegians. Lawlor almost succeeded
in obtaining a major immediately afte:~
wards, the Faugh’s goalie saving in the
nick of time.

Another success felt to Faughs, when
a lucky goal went through from a free.
Collegians were pressing at the finish, but

Faughs were deserving winners. Final
score:
Faughs - 6 goals 2 points.

Collegians - 3 goals 2 points,
